attributes.stem:
A 42-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-week history of a painful erythematous rash around his mouth and on his hands and feet. He has a history of alcohol dependence and reports poor dietary intake over the past six months. He does not take any regular medications. He noticed that his wound healing is slower than usual and has ongoing loose bowels without haematochezia or melena.

On examination, he appears unwell with vital signs within normal limits. There are symmetrical erythematous patches with scaling and crusting around his mouth, anus, and on his hands and feet. The rash spares his lips. There is notable hair loss on his head not typical of male-pattern baldness.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate treatment?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with characteristic features of acquired acrodermatitis enteropathica, a condition caused by zinc deficiency. The typical presentation includes periorificial and acral dermatitis, diarrhoea, alopecia and poor wound healing. In adults, this is usually secondary to inadequate dietary intake (poor diet or excessive alcohol consumption), malabsorption (inflammatory bowel disease or cystic fibrosis) or urinary loss (nephrotic syndrome).

Oral zinc supplementation is the definitive treatment for zinc deficiency. The recommended dose is 30-60mg of elemental zinc daily. Clinical improvement is typically seen within days to weeks of starting supplementation. The cause of the zinc deficiency - in this case, his excessive alcohol use - needs to be addressed to prevent further issues and to determine if there are concurrent nutritional deficiencies.

Oral fluconazole would be appropriate for fungal infections such as oral candidiasis, but would not address the underlying zinc deficiency causing the characteristic distribution of skin lesions. Oral candidiasis would typically also have the white cottage cheese-like lesions which is not present in this case.

Topical betamethasone might temporarily improve the appearance of the rash but would not address the underlying nutritional deficiency. Steroid use without treating the zinc deficiency could potentially worsen the condition. Perioral dermatitis may present with erythematous patches but is more associated with previous steroid use. It would not explain the rash found in other areas not explain the cause of his poor wound healing.

Oral vitamin B complex, while potentially beneficial for this patient given his alcohol use, would not treat the primary problem of zinc deficiency. The characteristic periorificial and acral distribution of the rash is not typical for vitamin B deficiencies. Thiamine deficiency typically presents with mental state changes, ataxia or altered sensation in the lower limbs which is not present in the stem.

Topical mupirocin would be appropriate for bacterial skin infections but would not address the underlying zinc deficiency causing the characteristic rash pattern.
learningPoints: Acquired acrodermatitis enteropathica presents with periorificial and acral dermatitis due to zinc deficiency.
Risk factors include malabsorption disorders, alcoholism, and inadequate dietary intake.
references: Ngan V, Gangakhedkar A, Oakley A. Acrodermatitis enteropathica. DermNet NZ. 2019. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/acrodermatitis-enteropathica
Wang L, Busbey S. Acquired Acrodermatitis Enteropathica.  New England Journa lof Medicine. 2005: 352 (11).  Available at: https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMicm030844
